title: Photo Mounting Options
subtitle: Photo corners, dry adhesives, PVA spot mounting, and full-float window mounting for scrapbooks
excerpt: Four approaches to mounting photographs in a scrapbook: archival photo corners, dry adhesive sheets, PVA spot mounting, and a float window mat. The key distinction is reversibility: which methods allow the photograph to be removed undamaged.
sourceNotes: Conservation mounting practice as documented in archival literature; no single PD canonical source.

glossaryTerms.definition: Small triangular sleeves made of paper or acetate that slip over each corner of a photograph and are adhesive on their back, holding the photograph without adhesive touching the image surface.
A mounting method that allows the mounted item to be removed in the future without damage, using adhesives that can be dissolved or mechanical fixings that can be released.
Mounting using materials that are acid-free, lignin-free, and chemically stable, preventing deterioration of photographs and paper over decades.

content.text: Reversible versus permanent: why it matters
A photograph stuck directly to a scrapbook page with adhesive cannot be removed without damage. For photographs that have value beyond the current scrapbook layout, a
reversible mount
means the photograph can be recovered intact if the scrapbook deteriorates or the layout is redesigned. Methods 1 and 2 below are reversible. Methods 3 and 4 are not.
Method 1: Archival photo corners
Pre-made
photo corners
are available in black, clear, and white variants. For
archival mounting
choose corners labelled acid-free; standard clear plastic corners often contain PVC, which off-gasses acids over time.
Place the photograph on the page in its intended position. Mark the corners lightly in pencil.
Peel the backing from each corner and press onto the page at the four marks. Allow to adhere for 30 seconds before inserting the photograph.
Slide each corner of the photograph into its sleeve. The photograph should sit flat and snug but not bent.

DIY corners: cut 2.5 cm squares of black scrapbooking paper. Fold diagonally. Fold both outer corners to the centre to form a small triangle with a pocket. Secure the back with a tiny dot of methyl-cellulose (which is fully reversible with water). Slide over the photograph corners and mount as above.
Method 2: Dry adhesive (acid-free)

Acid-free double-sided tape and dry adhesive sheets are available from scrapbooking suppliers. These apply clean and dry in seconds with no wet-glue mess. However, once applied to a photograph surface they are not fully reversible: the tape will remove cleanly from the mounting page if caught quickly, but will pull away paper fibres from a photographic print if left for any length of time.
Best use: mounting printed-at-home or copy prints where the original file still exists. Not recommended for unique original photographs.
Apply strips of acid-free double-sided tape to the back corners and centre of the photograph.
Peel the backing. Position on the page, align, then press firmly from the centre outward.
Method 3: PVA spot mounting
PVA spot mounting is permanent. Use only acid-free PVA to avoid the photograph yellowing over time.
Apply four small dots of acid-free PVA to the back of the photograph, one near each corner, keeping glue at least 5 mm from the edges.
Allow the dots to become slightly tacky (about 30 seconds).
Position the photograph on the page and press firmly. Protect with a sheet of clean cartridge paper and press under a heavy book for 10 minutes.
Spot mounting keeps the photograph slightly flexible. This reduces cracking if the page bends during use.
Method 4: Float window-mat mounting

A window mat is a separate card frame, cut with a rectangular opening, placed over the photograph. The photograph appears to float behind the frame. This is the most formal-looking option and gives the strongest visual presentation for a key photograph.
Mount the photograph to the scrapbook page first using photo corners (Method 1).
Cut a frame from a separate sheet of cartridge paper or card stock. The opening should be 3 to 4 mm smaller on each side than the photograph, so the frame overlaps the edges.
Score and fold the inner edges of the frame with a bone folder so the cut edges fold slightly back from the photograph surface, creating a small shadow line.
Stick the frame to the page using acid-free PVA or double-sided tape. Apply it to the outer edges of the frame only, keep it away from the window opening.

items.symptom: Photo corners slip off the page after a few weeks
items.cause: Adhesive on the corner backing was not fully pressed down
items.fix: Remove the corner. Clean any adhesive residue from the page. Apply a tiny dot of acid-free PVA to the corner back and re-press under weight.

items.symptom: PVA spot-mounted photograph buckles at the centre
items.cause: Photograph expanded on one face only due to moisture in the PVA
items.fix: Press under weight for longer: at least 30 minutes. For future mounts, allow the PVA dots to skin over more before pressing the photograph down.

items.symptom: Window mat frame opening is uneven
items.cause: The inner corners were not cut fully through on each pass
items.fix: Use a craft knife and metal ruler; cut each edge separately with the ruler as a guide. Make a second pass rather than trying to cut through thick card in one go.
